content.full_text:
State of California                                A-77-296

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 13, 1977


Mrs. Clara Link
555 South San Rafael Avenue
Pasadena, CA  91105

Dear Clara:

     Thank you for your letter regarding the position being
taken by the Los Angeles Board of Supervisors regarding the
review of school board Conflict of Interest Codes.  I am
sorry for not getting back to you sooner.  I was out of the
office the week your letter arrived, and since returning I
have been tied up in Commission meetings.

     It may interest you and Mr. Shatford to know that the
issue you raise has been discussed by the FPPC.  One of the
water districts whose Code was reviewable by the FPPC attempted
to insert a similar provision eliminating disclosure of
interests in companies which supply less than a stated amount
of goods and services to the district.  The Commission strongly
rejected this concept.  Interestingly, I was virtually the only
member of the Commission or the staff who saw any merit in the
concept whatever, and at most I would have supported a "de
minimus" paragraph setting a threshold of about $1,000 or
$2,000.  To set the threshold at $10,000 seems to me to be
high, and as I indicated, I was the only member of the Com-
mission who saw merit in having any threshold whatsoever.

     The FPPC has, in a sense, approved a model school dis-
trict Code, because a fair number of school districts across
county lines and therefore are subject to review of their
Codes by the Commission.  The model Code, of which a copy is
enclosed, requires disclosure of interests in all contractors
with the district.  The Commission has recently indicated
that it will approve provisions in Codes of relatively small
districts that would eliminate the disclosure requirement if
the contractor is a huge (more than $250 million in sales and
assets) company.  The theory behind this provision is that
the purchases of a small district could not have a significant
effect one way or the other on such a large company.  Such a
provision is, of course, quite different from the one dis-
cussed by Mr. Shatford, since a purchase of just under


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-6001 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6641

Mrs. Clara Link
June 13, 1977
Page Two


$10,000 would admittedly be insignificant in the case of a
large company, but could be highly significant in the case
of a small company.

Although it seems clear from the foregoing that the pro-
vision you described would not be approved in a Code reviewed
by the FPPC, you are of course aware that under Proposition 9
considerable authority is delegated to the boards of super-
visors and city councils when it comes to review of local
Codes. The FPPC has no authority to overrule the decision of
a local code reviewing body, although such decisions can
certainly be challenged in court. I am certain that a court
would approve the insertion of a "de minimus" concept into a
Conflict of Interest Code, but whether the provision in ques-
tion would be deemed so excessive as to amount to an abuse of
discretion I cannot say.

Because so much time has gone by since your letter
arrived, I have dictated a response today, even though
Del Spurlock, the head of our Conflict of Interest Division,
is out of town. If you or Mr. Shatford would like further
background and information on this subject, I would urge you
to contact Del, who will certainly be more familiar than I
am with the policies being followed by the Los Angeles Board
of Supervisors as well as by other local code reviewing
bodies. If you feel that I can be of any further assistance,
please let me know. Your longstanding and consistent support
of the Commission and Proposition 9 has been very much
appreciated.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

DHL:cjb

Enclosure

MRS. RICHARD M. LINK
555 SOUTH SAN RAFAEL AVENUE • PASADENA, CALIFORNIA 91105

May 24, 1977

Dan Lowenstein, Chairman PPFC
1100 K Street Mall
Sacramento, Ca. 94101

Dear Dan:

Yesterday I received a fascinating comment on
local level conflict of interest codes from Welter
Shatford II, a long-time member of the Pasadena
City College Board of Trustees.

Walter is an attorney, a dedicated public servant,
a man of integrity. He supports Prop. 9. He also
understands why some local office holders are convinced
that the requirements of local conflict of interest codes
are an "invasion of privacy." Walter gave me permission
to quote from his letter, with the understanding that
he is not seeking, nor does he want any publicity what-
soever, unless absolutely necessary. The reason: Walter
needs to retain as good a working relationship as
possible with his fellow board members -- some of
whom are apposed to Prop. 9.

-- from Shatford letter dated May 21, 1977:

"The conflict of interest law is working very
strangely. All the Boards in the County -- PCC for
example -- have had to submit codes selecting which
employees will have to comply with which sections of
the code. This also includes the Trustees.

"Confronted with hundreds of such codes the County
Board of Supervisors has come up with a model code. It
can change locally adopted codes...yesterday, after we
had adopted the model code on Thursday, we got a notice
that the Trustees only need report sales of supplies to
the district of $10,000 or over!

We were advised that if we had already adopted,
the Board of Supervisors would add the underlined and
we could ratify the action at our next meeting. In
short, we go through proposing but the Board of
Supervisors disposes and disposes in such a way as
to help every entrepreneur in sight!

"So much for conflict of interest."

In a phone conversation regarding the above, Walter
stressed the horrendous problem the Board of Supervisors
would have if each district in the County adopted
different codes. A uniform code does make sense, even
thought it may not be exactly what each individual
Board would prefer.

We also discussed whether it would be a good idea
for the FPPC to compare conflict of interest codes of
various Counties, correlate the findings and then sponsor
legislation for a uniform conflict of interest code --
or, at least draft a model code that would conform
with the intent of the law.

Walter believes, and I agree, that school boards
throughout the State should have similar guidelines
in order to eliminate erratic differences. School
Boards in one county should not be under onerous
restrictions while School Boards in another county
have such loose conflict guidelines as to be meaningless.

It seems to me it would be easier for school boards
(and other local jurisdictions) and for FPPC to comply
with and administer the law, if the FPPC would draft
a reasonable, workable model applicable throughout the State.

Everybody would know where they stood.

Much confusion would be eliminated.

Dan, I know that PPPC is deluged with "whatchadas"
-- most of them worthless. However, I believe that
Walter Shetford's comments do merit thoughtful con-
sideration.

Thank you for listening.

Sincerely yours,

Clara Lusk
